title: Knafeh Nabulsiyeh
subtitle: Shredded pastry with stretchy cheese and rose-scented sugar syrup
excerpt: Knafeh Nabulsiyeh is the Palestinian version of the Levantine knafeh: shredded kataifi pastry toasted in butter, pressed around a stretchy melting cheese, baked until golden, then drenched in cold sugar syrup scented with orange blossom water and finished with crushed pistachios.
sourceNotes: Knafeh is a Levantine pastry with documented roots in Nablus (now in the West Bank), where the use of akkawi or Nabulsi cheese distinguishes the local version. The dish appears in historical accounts of Palestinian and Syrian food from the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries. The technique and flavour combinations here follow the established Nabulsiyeh tradition.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Knafeh is best served immediately, straight from the oven. The cheese sets as it cools; reheating restores the stretch.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan: 180 degrees C; the pastry base should be deep amber when done

glossaryTerms.definition: Kataifi is a form of filo pastry extruded as fine vermicelli-like strands rather than flat sheets, used across Middle Eastern and Greek baking. It is sold fresh or frozen in packets. For knafeh it is pulled apart, tossed in butter, and pressed into a tin to form the crust. When baked, it becomes crisp and golden; under the cheese it absorbs some of the syrup and softens slightly.

Orange blossom water is a distillate made from the flowers of the bitter orange tree, used as a flavouring across Middle Eastern, North African, and some Mediterranean baking. It is used in small quantities: too much produces a soapy, medicinal flavour. In the syrup for knafeh it provides the floral high note that balances the richness of the cheese and butter.

content.text: Knafeh is served hot from the pan, inverted so the golden
base faces up, immediately drenched in cold sugar syrup scented with

. The cold syrup on hot pastry is part of the dish: the cheese inside stays molten and pulls in long strands as the first piece is lifted. Akkawi cheese is traditional; soak it overnight to reduce saltiness. Fresh mozzarella is the easiest substitute.

items.prepNote: thawed if frozen, pulled apart into loose strands
fresh, torn, excess water squeezed out

content.text: Combine the sugar and water in a small saucepan over medium heat. Stir until the sugar dissolves, then simmer without stirring for 8 minutes. Add the lemon juice and orange blossom water. Remove from the heat and leave to cool completely; the syrup should be cold by the time the knafeh comes out of the oven.

Preheat the oven to 200 degrees C (conventional). Generously butter a 30 cm round baking tin or a 23 cm tin for a deeper result. Pour the melted butter over the kataifi strands and work it in with your hands until every strand is coated. Divide the buttered pastry in half. Press one half into the base of the tin in an even layer, compressing it firmly. Spread the torn and drained mozzarella over the top in a thick, even layer. Press the remaining kataifi over the cheese, packing it firmly to seal the edges. Bake for 25 to 30 minutes, until the top is deep golden. Remove from the oven, immediately invert onto a large plate or board, and pour the cold syrup evenly over the hot pastry. Scatter with chopped pistachios and serve at once.
